skills_i_can_now_do: Write a counterclaim-aware claim using the 'Although X, Y because Z' frame (concede the other side's best point, hold my position, give a reason)
Make the reason directly ANSWER the objection instead of just adding another point for my side
Apply the 3-question checklist (concede? hold position? reason answers objection?) to test a claim
Spot fake concessions — knowing the word 'although' alone doesn't make a claim counterclaim-aware; it's what the sentence actually does
Diagnose and rewrite a one-sided draft by adding a concession and a reason that answers it
Write a counterclaim-aware claim on a brand-new topic without a frame, catching and fixing my own failed first attempt
terms_learned: Although X, Y because Z frame
where_i_struggled: My first instinct on the new topic (daylight saving) failed question 3 — I wrote a reason that just restated my side ('disrupts sleep') instead of answering the dark-mornings objection, and had to rethink it
Step 5 (A) tricked me for a moment — it had a real concession, but the reason didn't answer the objection
In Steps 7-9 my sentences were basically copies of the lesson's example / my own earlier sentence, since it was the same topic and side
Worry going forward: on a harder topic I might not always be able to think of a reason that truly answers the objection instead of dodging it
open_questions: On harder topics, how do I come up with a reason that genuinely answers the objection rather than dodging it?

skills_i_can_now_do: Spot the 'concede-and-COLLAPSE' trap — claims that concede and then surrender the position with phrases like 'it is hard to say' or 'it stays complicated'
Identify which claim concedes AND holds a side among options, including collapses dressed up with words like 'since'
Instantly catch a fake concession that concedes your OWN point ('a reason wearing an although costume') — the trap type that tricked me last lesson
Diagnose a collapsing draft and fix it by holding a side with a reason
Rewrite a weak conceding-but-collapsing draft into one that concedes, holds a side, and gives a reason
Write a concede-and-hold claim with no frame, choosing a trickier real objection and answering it (one-time hard choice vs. repeated yearly harm)
Use a strategy to find a reason that truly answers the objection: ask 'what would fix the conceded problem WITHOUT giving up my side?'
Apply that strategy on a brand-new topic (longer school year), catching my own dodge-reason ('it's expensive') and replacing it with one that actually answers the summer-slide objection
where_i_struggled: My first instinct on the new topic (longer school year) was my old dodge habit again — 'it's expensive' didn't answer the summer-slide objection, and I had to catch and rethink it
Step 6's feedback confused me a little about which side is conceding what — the fix sentence seemed to concede the arguer's own point depending on which side they're on
Most of the lesson (Steps 2–9) felt like a rerun of lesson 1 — same topic, same frame — so I wrote nearly the same sentence three times and didn't learn anything new from those steps

Today's third checklist question was just 'is there a reason?' instead of 'does the reason answer the objection?', which felt like the lesson lowered the bar instead of helping with my actual struggle
open_questions: Why did this lesson weaken the third checklist question to just 'is there a reason?' when answering the objection is the part I actually struggle with?
In a concession, which side is supposed to be conceding what — how do I keep straight whose point is being conceded when the positions flip?
Does my new strategy ('what fixes the conceded problem without giving up my side?') work reliably on harder topics, or did it just happen to work this time?

felt_repeated.what: Steps 2–9 felt like about 70% rerun of lesson 1: same daylight saving topic, same 'Although X, Y because Z' frame, same 3-question idea, and I wrote nearly the same claim sentence three times (Steps 7, 8, 9). The worked example's final sentence was almost word-for-word my final claim from last lesson.

skills_i_can_now_do: Stretch the concede-and-hold move from one sentence into a full POSITION / CONCEDE / ANSWER paragraph
Answer an objection with a FACT from the source plus a reason, not just a reason alone
Identify which of several conceding paragraphs actually ANSWERS the objection (vs. repeating the claim or changing the subject)
Spot the subject-change trap: a real fact that doesn't touch the objection at all
Diagnose a name-and-drop paragraph and pick the fix that answers the objection with a fact and reason
Choose and concede the critics' STRONGEST objection (targeted summer programs instead of a longer year) rather than copying the lesson's easier cost example
Apply my strategy ('what fixes the conceded problem without giving up my side?') to find a source-based answer to a hard objection
Write concede + answer sentences that stay on the objection's own terms (e.g., keeping the answer about low-income students when the objection is about low-income students)
Catch my own subject-change instinct before writing it down, faster than in earlier lessons
Run the 3-part check (position clear? conceded fairly? answered with fact + reason?) on my own writing and explain how it engages instead of dodging
Combine concede and answer moves into a full counterclaim paragraph
terms_learned: answer with a fact from the source

The topic (longer school year) was reused from the end of lesson 2, which felt like reheated leftovers while reading the first article
Step 5 felt almost too easy — same diagnosis twice in a row

felt_repeated.what: The core move is the same 'Although X, Y because Z' concede-and-hold from l01 and l02, just stretched into a paragraph (the lesson even says 'you are not learning a new move today'); the topic (longer school year) is literally reused from my end-of-l02 fresh-topic task; and the 'naming isn't answering' diagnosis was repeated four times within this lesson (Steps 3, 4, 5, 7), with Step 8 being Steps

skills_i_can_now_do: Tell the difference between an analytical (craft) claim and a summary — knowing a sentence can be accurate and still not be analysis
Recognize that putting the author's name in a sentence ('Chopin describes...') doesn't make it analysis — it's whether the sentence claims a choice and a purpose
Apply the craft-check questions (choice named? what is it doing? about craft not plot?) to test a claim
Identify the true analytical claim among options: spot play-by-play summary, reporting-a-choice-without-purpose, and empty opinion ('the story is good')
Spot the new trap: a deep-sounding theme/meaning claim that points to no author choice (theme claim vs. craft claim)
Diagnose a draft that names WHAT is described but not what the describing is DOING, and reject fixes that just add more summary or length
Write my own analytical claim with the frame, choosing a fresh choice (irony) instead of copying the lesson's example, and catching my own too-vague first draft ('uses irony to surprise the reader')
Rewrite a weak draft that only reports a choice (repetition of 'free') into one that claims what the choice is doing, and name which craft-check question the rewrite fixed
Transfer my testing habit from the concede/hold/answer checklist to a new checklist: it's what the sentence DOES, not the words it contains
where_i_struggled: The old-fashioned language in the Chopin story slowed me down ('bespoke repression,' 'forestall any less careful, less tender friend')
In Step 5, option (C) almost tricked me — a deep-sounding meaning claim with no choice attached; I was about to pick it before checking what choice it pointed to
Worry: in a real essay I'd probably write a C-type sentence (theme claim with no choice) and feel proud of it
My first mental draft in Step 7 ('Chopin uses irony to surprise the reader') was too vague — 'surprise the reader' didn't say to what end, and I had to rethink it
My Step 7 sentence ended up close to Step 5's answer (A) — I couldn't fully un-see that example once I read it
Before this lesson I'm pretty sure my 9th-grade 'analysis' was mostly summary with fancier words
open_questions: In a real essay without multiple-choice options in front of me, will I catch myself writing a deep-sounding theme claim with no author choice attached?
The excerpt stops suddenly at 'Free! Body and soul free!' — is there more to the story that I'm not getting?
Can I write an analytical claim that isn't leaning on an example sentence I already saw in the lesson?

skills_i_can_now_do: Know that a real craft claim needs TWO parts: a technique the author literally did (placement, repetition, image, point of view) AND the effect on the reader
Tell 'what the character feels' (content) apart from 'what the author does' (craft) — at least in theory
Recognize that quoting a word from the text isn't enough — the effect part can't just be the character's feeling
Know the effect has to land on the READER, not just describe what the character experiences
Pick out the real craft claim among options, including catching the quote-a-word-but-payoff-is-a-feeling trap
Spot the HALF-claim trap: a claim that names a real technique but has no effect (stops halfway)
Diagnose a feeling-report claim with an author verb glued on ('Chopin reveals...') and know the fix is naming what the author does plus the effect on the reader
Recognize point of view ('keeps us inside her thoughts') as a technique, which I hadn't thought of before
Catch my own first instinct when it matched the lesson's failed example ('uses the word free to show she feels free') and stop before writing it
Notice craft details in a re-read on my own (like the window description coming BEFORE she feels anything)
terms_learned: point of view as a technique
where_i_struggled: I understand the character-feeling vs. author-craft distinction in theory but am not sure yet if I can do it in practice
My first instinct in the write step was literally the failed second try from the worked example ('uses the word free to show she feels free') — I had to catch and redo it
Step 5's option (A) was a new trap for me — a half claim with real craft but no effect — and it made the question trickier
The excerpt is the same one from lesson 4 and still cuts off suddenly, which was annoying; I only learned the twist (the husband isn't dead) from a spoiler in a wrong answer choice
open_questions: Can I actually do the craft-vs-content distinction in practice, not just recognize it in theory?
What happens in the rest of 'The Story of an Hour' — the husband apparently isn't dead, so how does Mrs. Mallard react when he walks back in?

felt_repeated.what: The teach step felt like the same 'author's name isn't analysis' trap from lesson 4, just zoomed in — and Step 2 reused the exact same Chopin excerpt from lesson 4, which I re-read faster the second time.

skills_i_can_now_do: Recognize that a craft claim needs a stated EFFECT ON THE READER, not just a named device
Know that specific description of a device (quoting vivid details) is still just a label without an effect — 'specific ≠ effect'
Know that commenting on the writing itself ('look how detailed this paragraph is') is label-only, a compliment on the craft, not analysis
Distinguish effect on the CHARACTER from effect on the READER — the 'so' clause has to land on the reader
Spot the three traps that fill the effect slot: more description, a compliment on the craft, and an effect on the character
Identify which claim among options states a real reader effect, dodging the 'reader'-word decoy on purpose instead of by luck
Diagnose that a device-only claim ('Chopin uses irony') needs an effect added, not a second device, a definition, or plot summary

Run the check on my own claim: device I can point to, effect on the reader (not the character, not a compliment on the author's skill)
terms_learned: effect on the reader vs. effect on the character

skills_i_can_now_do: Extend device→effect analysis with a third beat: the WARRANT — why the effect matters to the author's PURPOSE (Device → Effect → Warrant)
Recognize that praise words like 'interesting' or 'nice' are the analysis version of my old dodge — they add words without touching the purpose
Spot a 'because wearing a warrant costume' — a 'because' that points backward at the effect (explaining why the effect happens) instead of forward at the purpose
Identify which option supplies a real warrant vs. more device, praise, or a subject-change fact
Diagnose a device+effect sentence missing its warrant and pick the fix that adds why-it-matters
Write a full device→effect→warrant sentence with the frame and check whether my 'because' reaches the purpose
Rewrite a praise-only draft ('a nice closing line') into a full analysis where the effect is my own idea
Choose my own device with no frame and write a device→effect→warrant analysis without copying the lesson's example (the side-by-side dollar amounts)
Read a brand-new article with 'device-goggles' and immediately spot the author's moves (old-view opening, comparisons, brutal numbers)
On a new text, deliberately skip the lesson's suggested example and write my own analysis, connecting the warrant to the article's overall purpose
Transfer my dodge-radar from argument writing to analysis: the warrant must actually CONNECT to purpose, just like a reason must actually ANSWER the objection
terms_learned: warrant (in analysis: why the effect matters to the author's purpose)
'because wearing a warrant costume' (a because that re-describes the effect instead of reaching the purpose)

skills_i_can_now_do: Understand what a device-chain is: linking two author's choices that build the SAME point, instead of naming one choice and stopping

Hunt for author's choices on a first pass of a brand-new source (noticing the weather text's opening move mirrors the recycling text's shape)

stack vs. chain (two choices pulling toward different ideas is a stack, not a chain)

skills_i_can_now_do: Name the three parts of analysis (author CHOICE, EFFECT on the reader, WARRANT for why it matters) and use them to tell analysis from summary
Recognize that a paragraph can be accurate, detailed, and smooth and still be summary

Catch the 'specific/accurate = analysis' trap and the 'the author describes' name-without-choice trap quickly
Predict a rubric score for a summary-only paragraph (smooth summary caps at 1 out of 2)

Catch my own vague effect-shaped-nothing phrasing ('helps the reader understand') and replace it with a concrete effect (stakes climb from routine measurements to life-and-death alerts)

skills_i_can_now_do: Apply the two revision moves: ADD what the reader needs, DELETE what is off-purpose even when it is true
Run the 3-question check: name the point, test each sentence against it, cut or add
Identify the 'true but off-purpose' sentence to cut when given a point (e.g., the drained-for-farms loss fact in a wildlife paragraph)
Identify which sentence to ADD to supply the missing HOW, rejecting on-topic-ish tempters like storm background that don't do work for the point
Diagnose a weak draft and pick the right fix (cut the off-purpose sentence, not add detail to it or move it)

Rewrite a wildlife paragraph, cutting a true-but-irrelevant coverage fact and explaining why it doesn't serve the point
Revise without a frame: spot the off-purpose history/attitude line and rewrite so the point + HOW + result all serve the point
Transfer the add/delete moves to a brand-new topic (interstates), cutting a true history fact and adding a bridging proof stat so the payoff sentence points at something
Connect 'true but off-purpose' to the earlier subject-change trap: real ≠ relevant, a real fact can have the wrong job
Apply the fact + reason principle from the counterargument lessons on my own initiative, adding source stats even when not required
Second-guess whether an added stat is slightly off-point (quarter-of-all-miles vs. goods only) and make a reasoned call to keep it
terms_learned: delete (what is off-purpose even when true)

Predict how a weak 'revision' would score when none of the three moves was actually done
Revise a flood-protection draft with my own wording and a different source detail instead of copying the displayed example answer
Write a fresh purpose-driven paragraph and run the check on my own writing, catching and deleting a filler sentence that repeats the point in vaguer words (dead weight, not just off-topic)
Revise for purpose 'cold' without the checklist in front of me: state the point first, keep process order, add a payoff detail
Spot multiple off-purpose sentences (two sneaky deletes) in a single draft
Pull the on-purpose facts out of a new source and ignore the ones that don't serve my chosen purpose
terms_learned: purpose check (serve the purpose? missing detail? order builds?)

Not 100% sure whether adding TWO details (sponge + Mississippi stat) in my Step 7 revision is over-stuffing or fine — I think it's fine since both serve flood protection, but I can't confirm

open_questions: Is adding two on-purpose details to one paragraph over-stuffing, or fine as long as both serve the purpose?

felt_repeated.what: The individual moves are old — this lesson is basically lesson 10 (add/delete) plus lesson 11 (reorder) smashed together; the only new part is doing all three at once plus the warning about quitting after the obvious fix. Also, Step 7 reused the exact draft whose finished answer was shown in Step 3, repeating the copying problem from lessons 3, 8, and 13.

skills_i_can_now_do: Map a multi-source pool before writing: read every source, label each one's claim/objection, and give each source a job
Connect the con source to counterclaim work: recognize that the con source basically IS the objection I have to answer
Spot the one-source grab error (a draft that never opens the second source, leaving an unanswered counterclaim) — in both directions, pro-only and con-only
Distinguish real mapping from just reading both sources: know that 'I looked at both' isn't mapping unless each source gets a labeled job
Choose my OWN objection to answer instead of copying the worked example's, picking the strongest one (the toll-before-transit timing objection) because it attacks the pro side's best point
Reject an almost-too-easy objection when the pro source already half-answers it (the low-income one with discounts)
Run the pool check on my own map (every source checked? job named for each? objection to answer named?)
Know that 'more quotes from the same source' doesn't fix a one-source problem — more quotes doesn't equal more analysis
terms_learned: READ, LABEL, DECIDE, PICK, then write
where_i_struggled: The daylight saving 'con' source isn't a clean con — it partly AGREES the switching is the harm and proposes regional choice instead of opposing source 1's plan, which makes labeling its job harder; the lesson didn't warn me the con source might partly agree

Steps 7, 8, and 9 had me write basically the same pool map three times in a row on the same two sources, so I was mostly rephrasing/transcribing instead of thinking — same repeat problem as lessons 3, 8, and 13
Step 4's worked example pre-wrote the answer to the writing steps (it literally stated each source's job), so I worried I was just remembering that paragraph instead of figuring anything out
open_questions: How do I label a source's job when the 'con' source isn't a clean con — when it partly agrees with the pro source and proposes a middle path (like regional choice) instead of opposing the plan?
When I get to the writing steps after a worked example that pre-writes the answer, am I actually figuring anything out or just remembering the example's paragraph?

felt_repeated.what: The congestion pricing pro source reappeared with the same FHWA 3-trillion-miles and EPA transportation-emissions stats from around lessons 9–10; also, Steps 7–9 repeated the same pool map three times, the same worked-example-gives-away-the-answer problem I hit in lessons 3, 8, and 13

skills_i_can_now_do: Write a synthesis claim from multiple sources: my own position built from the whole set of sources, not a report of one source
Recognize that a synthesis claim = my counterclaim-aware claim + checking that more than one source shows up in it
Spot the 'Source 1 says X' summary trap — a report of a source is not a claim, even when it sounds thoughtful
Identify the real synthesis claim among options, catching giveaway openers like 'The first source explains that...'
Spot the trap fix that summarizes BOTH sources but still reports instead of taking a position — draws on the set but fails the 'is it MY position?' check
Fill the 'The sources together show that ___ because ___ and ___' frame with a position, a source-1 fact, and the reframed source-2 objection
Run the 3-part synthesis check (my position? draws on more than one source? arguable?) on my own claim
Use the critics' own admission ('the harm may come from the switching itself') against them to strengthen my side

Reframe an objection so it stops attacking my side (the trade-off worry is about WHICH clock, not about keeping the switch) — reusing my old strategy 'what fixes the conceded problem without giving up my side?'
Write a fresh synthesis claim from a different angle (states choose their fixed time based on the critics' regional point) instead of just rewording the shown answer
Slow down on trap options that resemble ones that fooled me before (like lesson 2's fake concession) instead of rushing
terms_learned: summary vs. claim trap ('Source 1 says X')
where_i_struggled: Step 4 handed me the best possible synthesis claim, then Steps 7-9 asked me to write one on the SAME topic — hard to un-see the shown answer, so my Step 7 claim was basically the Step 4 answer with a car-crash detail added; same copying problem I hit in lessons 3, 8, and 15
In Step 7 the only real thinking I did was picking WHICH health fact to use and noticing the critics' admission — I didn't discover the reframe myself

felt_repeated.what: The core move felt like a repeat of the concede-and-hold claim I've been doing since lesson 1 — the only new part is that the concession and position come from different sources and I check that both sources show up. skills_i_can_now_do: Weave two sources into ONE point instead of listing them one at a time ('book report' pattern: Source A says X, Source B says Y, both matter)
Apply the four-step process (READ, PICK, CONNECT, CHECK) with CONNECT — one fact confirms, answers, or targets the other — as the core move
Identify a woven point vs. two glued summaries vs. a point that ignores one source entirely

Spot the subtler 'parallel lanes' trap: two facts that both support the same conclusion but never touch each other is NOT weaving — the facts must relate to EACH OTHER, not just both point at the conclusion
Spot that a connector word like 'and' alone doesn't make a weave (like 'although' alone doesn't make a concession)
Diagnose a listed two-source draft and pick the fix that connects the facts on one point (not adding more sources, more quotes, or reordering)
Write a woven two-source point using the frame (my point / fact 1 / fact 2 / why they build one claim), using one source's concession as ammunition for my claim
Run the 3-question weave check (both sources in same point? connected not listed? one claim?) on my own writing
Write a fresh woven point without a frame, connecting one source's cost to the other source's hidden cost (replacement relationship)
Weave in either direction — argue the critics' side using the same two sources, making the mismatch between a universal cost and a specific harm the point itself
Flag usable facts while reading a brand-new source set (congestion pricing) before writing
Refuse the ready-made example the prompt hands me and force myself to weave a different pair of facts on the new topic
terms_learned: parallel lanes (two facts supporting the same claim without touching each other)
where_i_struggled: Steps 5 and 7's wrong options were too obviously wrong — not tempting at all (same complaint as lesson 12's steps 4–5)
Almost picked C in Step 6 because 'two reasons for one side' felt like it built one claim — the parallel-lanes trap nearly fooled me
Unsure in Step 8 whether using the critics' concession as my source-2 fact was 'cheating' vs. weaving the harm with the COST like the worked example — no teacher to ask, went with mine
Grading my own work with my own checklist always passes, which makes me suspicious of myself (same weird feeling as lesson 13's Step 8)
Step 12's prompt literally handed me the example woven answer on the 'do it yourself' step — same annoyance as lessons 12 and 13 — so I had to force a different pair of facts
Rereading the same longer-school-year sources for the third-ish time was a little boring, though knowing where the facts are let me focus on the new move
open_questions: Is using one source's concession (the critics' admission) as my 'fact' from that source cheating, or a legitimate weave — or did the lesson want me to weave the harm with the COST like the worked example?
Is a woven 'point' allowed to be one monster sentence doing a lot of work, or should it be two or three sentences that still connect? The lesson never says.
How do I trust my own checklist results when grading my own writing always passes — is there a way to check myself that isn't rigged?

skills_i_can_now_do: Read two sources with the 'how do they relate?' question in mind, finding where they AGREE (including surprising agreements like both accepting low-income discounts) and where they CLASH
Name a sources' relationship in one sentence: agreement + clash + the real disagreement, instead of just labeling them 'pro' and 'con' or saying 'they disagree'
Spot that 'the two sources disagree' is an empty label — like 'the author uses words' — technically true but says nothing
Identify which of several 'Both sources...' sentences actually names BOTH the agreement and the clash (vs. only half), reading what each sentence DOES rather than its surface words
Catch that a detailed, specific option can still be incomplete — specificity isn't the same as completeness
Diagnose a list-plus-vague-label draft ('one supports, one opposes, different views') and know it needs the relationship named, not more quotes, more length, or picking a winner
Recognize that picking a winner between sources is arguing, not synthesizing — a different job
Pinpoint the REAL disagreement beneath a surface clash (e.g., timing and trust about whether toll money reaches buses, or hidden cost everyone pays vs. visible cost hitting smaller paychecks)
Run the relationship check (agreement named? clash named? real disagreement pinpointed?) on my own sentences

Deliberately earn my own angle instead of recycling the worked example's answer when the same source set repeats, generating three different relationship angles (timing/trust, who-pays, downtown vitality)
Use one source's own idea against the other's (supporters' 'hidden cost' vs. critics' 'bigger bite') — synthesis instead of copying
Connect the agreement-and-clash move to concede-and-hold: it's the same move but between two AUTHORS instead of inside my own claim
terms_learned: naming the relationship (where sources agree and where they clash)
the real disagreement (sources can agree on the facts and differ only on what to do about them)
where_i_struggled: By Step 10 I was scraping for fresh angles — Steps 8, 9, and 10 are the same task on the same source set three times in a row, and I'd already spent my best original angle on Step 8
Step 9 forced a FRESH sentence different from Step 8, which was annoying since my best original angle was already used
My Step 8 sentence ran a little long — a known habit of mine (my lesson 17 rewrite ran long too) — though I judged every piece was doing a job
Step 4's worked example basically hands me the answer for Steps 8–10 since it uses the same source set, so I had to ban myself from reusing 'who pays / fairness' as my main answer
Step 5 was answered for me by Step 4's worked example modeling almost the exact same sentence
open_questions: Does naming the agreement AND the clash fully answer my lesson-17 question about labeling a source that isn't a clean 'con' — can I always avoid the pro/con box by saying what a source accepts and what it fights about?
When a lesson reuses one source set across the worked example and all the write steps, how many genuinely different relationship angles am I expected to find before it becomes recycling?
Is my Step 8 sentence too long, or is length fine as long as every piece is doing a job?

skills_i_can_now_do: Plan a synthesis essay by POINTS instead of by sources (avoid the paragraph-per-source trap)

skills_i_can_now_do: Assemble the whole argument-essay pipeline at once: synthesis claim, weave both sources, concede and answer, plan first, then build the full essay

terms_learned: assembly lesson (doing all the moves at once, nothing new)

skills_i_can_now_do: Take a cold cross-text gate test with no worked example on a brand-new topic (congestion pricing) with nothing to copy from

skills_i_can_now_do: Use a 'warrant' — a sentence explaining why a device's effect serves the author's purpose — recognizing it as basically lesson 7's 'significance' move under a new name
